business_goals: Optimize road traffic and provide tools for efficient traffic control
Enable fast response to road accidents or threats using real-time analysis
Provide efficient route planning and optimization based on dynamic traffic data
Integrate existing and new sensor infrastructure seamlessly
technical_constraints: Need for interoperability with diverse subsystems
architectural_drivers: Modifiability (Integration of new sensors and event types)

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Generic architectural views (ArchiMate) were available but lacked constructs to describe scenario influence on components or capture specific design decisions. Some specifications were missing or decisions had not yet been made.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What is the platform for this component (e.g., PostgreSQL, Linux, IIS)?
Is the component logic fixed or customizable?
What is the query granularity (bulk, low, medium, high)?
What communication type is used (synchronous, asynchronous)?

hardware_view_description: Data originates from a network of fixed sensors (video detectors, acoustic sensors, inductive loops)
Data includes on-board GPS receivers installed in vehicles
End-user interaction via network or mobile interfaces
runtime_constraints: Real-time performance requirements for traffic analysis
High volume of data feeds from sensors
Interoperability with various sensor types and client modules

questions: How are transactions distributed across the distinct repositories (Static, Traffic, Event)?
How are ontology definitions synchronized with database dictionaries?
Is the query granularity optimized for bulk updates vs. individual reads?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: New sensor types and sensor processing software will be integrated in <6 days>
Dynamic Map will be capable of accepting feeds from up to 1000 sensors occurring every <60> sec
Dynamic Map will be capable of processing up to <200> read queries per second
New map features can be introduced and made discoverable with built-in functionality
Updating of data defining the road network can cause a loss of at most 10 values of a single monitoring parameter
Sensor failures will be detected in a configurable period ranging from 3 to 20 minutes
New event types and corresponding customizable detection routines can be introduced in 1 day
System will be able to accommodate and interpret location data from 5000 GPS tracker units updated every 5 sec
A Dynamic Map component can be ported to another platform within 20 days

architectural_approaches.description: This architectural style centralizes data management within a shared, persistent data store (repository) that is accessed by multiple independent components. Components interact primarily through the repository, by either depositing data into it or retrieving data from it, rather than directly communicating with each other. The system appears to utilize distinct repositories (Static, Traffic, Event) to manage various data types, supporting the collection and analysis of high volumes of sensor data.

addressed_attributes.rationale: The repository style enhances modifiability by decoupling components. New components (e.g., new sensor types, analysis modules) can be added or existing ones modified without impacting others, as long as they adhere to the repository's data schema and access interfaces. This facilitates the integration of new sensors and event types.

While not inherently a performance driver, a well-designed data-centric system with optimized data structures, indexing, and potentially distributed storage (as suggested by 'distinct repositories' and questions about 'transaction distribution') can be tuned for high throughput and real-time response, crucial for handling high volumes of sensor data and dynamic traffic analysis.

Centralizing data in a repository simplifies data management concerns like backup, recovery, and consistency. Data integrity can be enforced at the repository level, contributing to system stability and failure detection through consistent data states.

For developers, interacting with a single, consistent data source through well-defined interfaces can improve the usability of the system's data layer, making it easier to build applications and analysis tools.

By abstracting the data storage mechanism behind repository interfaces, the system can achieve a degree of portability. The underlying database technology could potentially be swapped or adapted to different platforms with less impact on the data-consuming components, although the style itself doesn't guarantee full system portability.

scenarios.scenario_text: New sensor types and sensor processing software will be integrated in <6 days>
Dynamic Map will be capable of accepting feeds from up to 1000 sensors occurring every <60> sec
Dynamic Map will be capable of processing up to <200> read queries per second
New map features can be introduced and made discoverable with built-in functionality
Updating of data defining the road network can cause a loss of at most 10 values of a single monitoring parameter
Sensor failures will be detected in a configurable period ranging from 3 to 20 minutes
New event types and corresponding customizable detection routines can be introduced in 1 day
System will be able to accommodate and interpret location data from 5000 GPS tracker units updated every 5 sec
A Dynamic Map component can be ported to another platform within 20 days
Ensure data consistency across Static, Traffic, and Event repositories during high-volume, concurrent updates and reads, preventing stale or conflicting data.
Detect and report critical traffic anomalies (e.g., congestion, accident) within 5 seconds of the relevant sensor data being ingested into the system.

scenario_reference.text: Detect and report critical traffic anomalies (e.g., congestion, accident) within 5 seconds of the relevant sensor data being ingested into the system.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with high-volume data ingestion and processing.
analysis_records.stimulus: Ingestion of sensor data indicating a critical traffic anomaly.
analysis_records.response_measure: Anomaly detected and reported within 5 seconds.
architectural_decisions: Centralized data storage in distinct repositories (Static, Traffic, Event).
Components interact with data primarily via repository interfaces.
Anomaly detection logic operates on data retrieved from these repositories.
analysis_records.reasoning: The 5-second latency for anomaly detection and reporting is heavily dependent on the performance of the data-centric architecture. This involves the sum of latencies for data ingestion into repositories, data retrieval for processing, execution of anomaly detection algorithms, and reporting the anomaly. The 'Data-Centric / Repository Style' can enable high throughput through optimized data layers, but also introduces potential bottlenecks if repositories are not adequately provisioned or if cross-repository operations are inefficient. The distinct repositories offer modularity but pose challenges for ensuring timely consistency and efficient querying across them.

risks.description: High volume sensor data ingestion could overwhelm the write capacity of the Traffic/Event repositories, leading to delays beyond 5 seconds for anomaly detection.
risks.rationale: The data-centric approach's performance is highly dependent on the underlying repository's write capabilities. If not optimized for high ingestion rates (e.g., 1000 sensors/60s, 5000 GPS/5s), the system will struggle to meet the 5-second ingestion-to-detection window.

risks.description: Complex anomaly detection algorithms operating on large datasets from repositories might exceed the 5-second processing window.
risks.rationale: Retrieving potentially large volumes of data for analysis, performing complex computations, and storing results back to the Event repository can be computationally intensive and time-consuming, especially without efficient indexing or in-memory processing capabilities.

risks.description: Ensuring data consistency and efficient access across distinct repositories (e.g., Traffic and Static data) within 5 seconds for anomaly detection could be challenging.
risks.rationale: While distinct repositories offer modularity, correlating data across them (e.g., traffic data with static road network data) can introduce overheads related to distributed queries, data synchronization, and ensuring a consistent view of data for real-time anomaly detection.

risks.description: The reporting mechanism's latency might consume too much of the 5-second window, even if detection is fast.
risks.rationale: Even if an anomaly is detected promptly and stored, the time taken for the reporting component to retrieve this information from the Event repository and disseminate it to end-users or other systems can push the total response time beyond the 5-second limit.

non_risks.description: The centralized nature of the repository style inherently supports robust data integrity mechanisms, beneficial for reliable anomaly detection.
non_risks.rationale: By centralizing data management, the architecture facilitates the enforcement of data consistency, schema validation, and transactional integrity at the repository level. This reduces the risk of anomaly detection being based on corrupted or inconsistent input data, improving the reliability of the detection process.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Repository Read Latency (for anomaly detection queries)
Anomaly Detection Algorithm Complexity and Efficiency

tradeoff_points.parameter: Level of Data Consistency Across Repositories
conflicting_attributes: Performance (negative for strong consistency in distributed setups)

tradeoff_points.parameter: Data Granularity and Retention Period in Repositories
conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (positive for more raw data, enabling new analyses)
Performance (negative for larger data volumes, impacting query speed)

tradeoff_points.parameter: Degree of Decoupling Between Distinct Repositories
conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (positive for independent evolution of data types)
Performance (negative for cross-repository queries, requiring more complex coordination)
